sources.title: Signs and Symptoms of Stroke
facts: Sudden face, arm, speech, vision, balance or severe-headache symptoms require immediate emergency activation.
A transient symptom does not remove the need for emergency assessment.

sources.title: Warning Signs of a Heart Attack
facts: Chest pressure, squeezing, fullness or pain, upper-body discomfort, shortness of breath, cold sweat, nausea or lightheadedness can be heart-attack warning signs.
Emergency services should be called rather than delaying or self-transporting.

sources.title: 2024 American Heart Association and American Red Cross Guidelines for First Aid
facts: Emergency services should be activated for acute non-traumatic chest pain.
Aspirin is conditional on alertness and absence of allergy or prior contraindication; the guideline does not support substituting unspecified traditional rescue pills for emergency care.

sources.title: First Aid for Seizures
facts: Keep the person safe, turn them on their side when appropriate, time the seizure, do not restrain them and do not put anything in their mouth.
Emergency help is required for a seizure lasting more than five minutes, repeated seizures, trouble breathing or waking, first seizure, injury, pregnancy or water exposure.

sources.title: Definition and Facts of Urinary Retention
facts: Sudden inability to urinate with a full bladder is acute urinary retention.
Acute urinary retention can be life-threatening and requires emergency medical treatment right away.

sources.title: Gastrointestinal bleeding
facts: Black tarry stool, large amounts of blood in stool, vomiting blood or coffee-ground material, dizziness, chest or breathing symptoms, or severe abdominal pain with bleeding require emergency care.

sources.title: Suicide questions and answers
facts: Suicidal or self-harm thoughts indicate severe emotional distress and warrant professional help.
If immediate danger is present, contact emergency or crisis services and do not leave the person alone.

sources.title: Poison Help: What You Can Do
facts: Suspected poisoning requires immediate contact with poison experts; collapse, seizure, breathing difficulty or inability to wake requires emergency services.

sources.title: Poisoning first aid
facts: Do not induce vomiting unless directed by poison control or a health professional.
Do not give an unconscious person anything by mouth or use cure-all antidotes; do not wait for symptoms before seeking poison advice.

sources.title: Fainting first aid
facts: Assess responsiveness and breathing, use the recovery position when appropriate, monitor changes and activate emergency help for concerning features.
The guidance does not support routine oral sugar water while consciousness and swallowing safety are uncertain.

sources.title: Asthma Attack
facts: Call emergency services when prescribed relief medicine does not relieve an attack or breathing remains very hard.
Serious signs include drowsiness or confusion, blue skin or lips and severe breathing difficulty.

sources.title: Abdominal pain
facts: Sudden sharp pain, rigid or tender abdomen, inability to pass stool with vomiting, blood, pregnancy, breathing difficulty or severe associated symptoms require urgent or emergency assessment.

sources.title: Diabetic Ketoacidosis
facts: DKA is life-threatening; fast deep breathing, fruity breath, persistent vomiting, breathing difficulty and multiple DKA symptoms require emergency care.

sources.title: Case Management: Malaria Diagnosis and Treatment
facts: Suspected malaria requires prompt diagnostic testing and recommended antimalarial treatment; malaria can be severe, fatal and a medical emergency.

boundaries: These sources validate emergency recognition and escalation boundaries, not traditional disease mechanisms, pattern differentiation, formulas or dosages.
Country-specific emergency numbers, poison lines and medicine availability must be localized before any product use.
A supported red-flag sentence does not make adjacent treatment text eligible.
All source treatment and home-action text remains blocked in this candidate layer.
